file_name: OTISWORLDWIDECORP_04_03_2020-EX-10.4-INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY AGREEMENT by and among UNITED TECHNOLOGIES CORPORATION, OTIS WORLDWIDE CORPORATION and CARRIER ~1.txt

perturbation.original_text: If any term, provision, covenant or restriction of this Agreement is held by a court of competent jurisdiction or other authority to be invalid, void or unenforceable, the remainder of the terms, provisions, covenants and restrictions of this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect and shall in no way be affected, impaired or invalidated so long as the economic or legal substance of the transactions contemplated hereby is not affected in any manner materially adverse to any Party hereto. Upon such a determination, the Parties shall negotiate in good faith to modify this Agreement so as to effect the original intent of the Parties as closely as possible in a mutually acceptable manner in order that the transactions contemplated hereby be consummated as originally contemplated to the fullest extent possible.
perturbation.changed_text: If any term, provision, covenant or restriction of this Agreement is held by a court of competent jurisdiction or other authority to be invalid, void or unenforceable, the entire Agreement shall be considered void and null. Upon such a determination, the Parties are under no obligation to renegotiate or modify this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the severability clause to state that the entire agreement becomes void if any part is unenforceable contradicts the principle of severability, potentially conflicting with state contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles regarding severability. The Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) and common law in most states favor severing unenforceable provisions to preserve the contract's overall validity.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating the entire agreement is void and there's no obligation to renegotiate, the modified text contradicts UCC § 2-302 (and similar common law principles) which allows courts to sever unconscionable clauses to avoid invalidating the entire contract. The modified text removes this option, potentially violating standard contract severability doctrines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by removing the severability clause and stating the entire agreement is void if any part is unenforceable, directly opposing the principle outlined in UCC § 2-302. The original text maintained the agreement's validity as much as possible, whereas the changed text invalidates the entire contract, conflicting with the court's ability to 'enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1.

perturbation.original_text: Without limitation, Otis and Carrier as promptly as reasonably practicable (but in any case within six (6) months of the Effective Time) shall cause each member of, respectively, the Otis Group and the Carrier Group having a corporate name that includes any of the United Technologies Trademarks to apply to change its corporate name to a name that does not include any of the United Technologies Trademarks, including, within six (6) months of the Effective Time, by making any legal filings in each relevant jurisdiction necessary to effect such change worldwide.
perturbation.changed_text: Without limitation, Otis and Carrier as promptly as reasonably practicable (but in any case within one (1) month of the Effective Time) shall cause each member of, respectively, the Otis Group and the Carrier Group having a corporate name that includes any of the United Technologies Trademarks to apply to change its corporate name to a name that does not include any of the United Technologies Trademarks, including, within one (1) month of the Effective Time, by making any legal filings in each relevant jurisdiction necessary to effect such change worldwide.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the timeframe to one month might conflict with regulations related to corporate name changes in some jurisdictions, as this may be insufficient time to do it practically or legally.
perturbation.contradicted_law: State corporate law regarding name changes. Varies by state, but often requires specific procedures and timelines.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware General Corporation Law § 391 outlines procedures for amending a corporate charter, including name changes. While not explicitly dictating a minimum timeframe, requiring compliance within one month might be deemed commercially unreasonable if it doesn't allow sufficient time for required filings and approvals, especially across multiple jurisdictions as required by the original agreement. State laws vary greatly, and this may prove difficult to follow, especially considering the global nature of the company.

perturbation.justification: The change from six months to one month for corporate name changes introduces a potential legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that requiring compliance within one month might be deemed commercially unreasonable if it doesn't allow sufficient time for required filings and approvals, especially across multiple jurisdictions. The original agreement requires this across multiple jurisdictions, which could make the one-month deadline practically impossible, thus unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, although primarily focused on the structure of close corporations, highlights the necessity of compliance with Delaware corporation law, and amending a corporation charter, including name changes, may not be possible in 1 month. This makes the change a legal contradiction.
